lessons.title: Acids and Bases — Properties and pH

content.body: Acids and bases are two of the most important classes of chemical compounds. They are found everywhere — in food, medicines, cleaning products, and our own bodies.

Arrhenius definitions:
Acid: produces H⁺ ions in water. e.g. HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻
Base: produces OH⁻ ions in water. e.g. NaOH → Na⁺ + OH⁻

Brønsted-Lowry:
Acid: a proton (H⁺) donor
Base: a proton (H⁺) acceptor

pH Scale (0–14):
pH 0–6: Acidic (lower = more acidic)
pH 7: Neutral (pure water)
pH 8–14: Alkaline/Basic (higher = more alkaline)

Strong acids: HCl (hydrochloric), H₂SO₄ (sulphuric), HNO₃ (nitric)
Strong bases: NaOH (sodium hydroxide), KOH (potassium hydroxide)

content.title: Common Acids and Bases in Daily Life
content.body:
Acids:
• Lemon juice / vinegar: pH 2-3 (citric acid, acetic acid)
• Stomach acid: pH 1-2 (hydrochloric acid)
• Fizzy drinks: pH 3-4 (carbonic acid)
• Rain water: pH ~5.6 (carbonic acid from CO₂)

Bases:
• Baking soda: pH ~8 (sodium bicarbonate)
• Soap: pH 9-10
• Ammonia cleaning solution: pH 11
• Bleach: pH 12

Neutralisation reaction:
Acid + Base → Salt + Water
HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O
H₂SO₄ + 2KOH → K₂SO₄ + 2H₂O

Indicators tell us if a solution is acidic/neutral/basic:
Litmus: red in acid, blue in alkali
Universal indicator: full range of colours
phenolphthalein: colourless in acid, pink in alkali

questions.question: What pH is neutral?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral — pure water has pH 7.

questions.question: An acid produces which ion in water?
questions.explanation: Acids donate protons (H⁺ ions) in water.

questions.question: What is the product of acid + base reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water.

questions.question: Stomach acid has pH of about 1-2. This means it is:
questions.explanation: pH 1-2 is at the very acidic end of the pH scale.

questions.question: Litmus paper turns RED in:
questions.explanation: Red litmus stays red in acid; blue litmus turns red in acid. Litmus turns blue in alkali.

questions.question: Which of these is a strong base?
questions.explanation: Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) is a strong base used in drain cleaners, soap making, and paper production.

questions.question: HCl + NaOH → ?
questions.explanation: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O (salt sodium chloride + water).

questions.question: Universal indicator is used to:
options: Give the exact pH of a solution
Show if a reaction is complete
questions.answer: Give the exact pH of a solution
questions.explanation: Universal indicator changes through a range of colours corresponding to different pH values.

questions.question: Acid rain has a pH of about 4. It is caused by:
options: SO₂ and NOₓ from burning fossil fuels
questions.answer: SO₂ and NOₓ from burning fossil fuels
questions.explanation: SO₂ and NOₓ from industrial/vehicle emissions dissolve in rainwater to form sulphuric and nitric acids.

questions.question: A Brønsted-Lowry base is:
questions.explanation: Brønsted-Lowry: acid = proton (H⁺) donor; base = proton (H⁺) acceptor.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: The Periodic Table is the most important tool in chemistry. It organises all 118 known elements by atomic number, and reveals patterns in their properties. It was developed by Dmitri Mendeleev in 1869.

Organisation of the Periodic Table:
Periods (rows): elements in the same row have the same number of electron shells
Groups (columns): elements in the same column have the same number of outer electrons → similar chemical properties

Group 1: Alkali Metals (Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr)
Group 7: Halogens (F, Cl, Br, I, At)
Group 0/18: Noble Gases (He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn)
Middle section: Transition Metals (Fe, Cu, Zn, etc.)

content.title: Group 1 — Alkali Metals
content.body:
Properties of Group 1 metals:
• Soft metals that can be cut with a knife
• React vigorously with water, producing hydrogen gas and an alkali
• 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂
• Reactivity INCREASES going DOWN the group (outer electron farther from nucleus, easier to lose)
• Stored in oil to prevent reaction with air/moisture

content.title: Group 7 — Halogens
content.body:
Properties of Group 7 (non-metals):
• F₂: pale yellow gas (most reactive)
• Cl₂: green-yellow gas
• Br₂: red-brown liquid
• I₂: grey-black solid (purple vapour)
• Reactivity DECREASES going DOWN the group
• Form salts with metals: 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl
• More reactive halogen displaces less reactive: Cl₂ + 2KBr → 2KCl + Br₂

Group 0 (Noble Gases): He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn.
They are extremely unreactive (inert) because they have full outer electron shells.
He is used in balloons. Ar is used in light bulbs. Ne is used in neon signs.

questions.question: Who developed the Periodic Table?
questions.explanation: Dmitri Mendeleev arranged elements by atomic mass and predicted properties of undiscovered elements in 1869.

questions.question: Elements in the same GROUP have:
questions.explanation: Elements in the same group have the same number of electrons in their outer shell, giving similar chemical properties.

questions.question: Which group are the alkali metals?
questions.explanation: Group 1 contains the alkali metals: Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr.

questions.question: Going DOWN Group 1, reactivity:
questions.explanation: Going down Group 1, the outer electron is farther from the nucleus and easier to lose → more reactive.

questions.question: Why are noble gases unreactive?
options: They have a full outer electron shell
questions.answer: They have a full outer electron shell
questions.explanation: Noble gases have 8 outer electrons (He has 2) — a full stable shell means no tendency to react.

questions.question: What is produced when sodium reacts with water?
questions.explanation: 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂↑. Sodium hydroxide (alkali) and hydrogen gas are produced.

questions.question: Going DOWN Group 7, reactivity:
questions.explanation: Going down Group 7, halogens become less reactive. Fluorine is most reactive, iodine least.

questions.question: Chlorine reacts with potassium bromide solution. What happens?
options: Nothing — Cl₂ is less reactive than Br₂
questions.explanation: More reactive halogens displace less reactive ones. Cl₂ is above Br₂ in reactivity, so Cl₂ displaces Br₂.

questions.question: What is the state of bromine at room temperature?
questions.explanation: Bromine (Br₂) is a red-brown liquid at room temperature — it is the only non-metal liquid element.

questions.question: Argon gas is used in light bulbs because:
options: It is cheap and inert — won't react with the filament
questions.answer: It is cheap and inert — won't react with the filament
questions.explanation: Argon is a noble gas — completely unreactive. This prevents the tungsten filament from burning.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: Moving across a period (left to right), atomic number increases by 1. Properties change systematically. Moving down a group, atoms have more electron shells.

Trends across Period 2/3:
Atomic radius: decreases across period (more protons pull electrons closer)
Ionisation energy: generally increases across period
Metallic character: decreases (metals on left, non-metals right)
Electronegativity: increases across period

Trends down a group:
Atomic radius: increases (more shells)
Ionisation energy: decreases (outer electrons further from nucleus)
Reactivity: metals increase, non-metals decrease

content.title: Group I — Alkali Metals
content.body:
Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs — all have 1 outer electron
Reactivity increases down group: K > Na > Li
Reaction with water: 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂
Potassium: vigorous, lilac flame; Sodium: vigorous, orange flame
Products: metal hydroxide (alkali) + hydrogen
All stored in oil to prevent reaction with air/water

content.title: Group VII — Halogens
content.body:
F, Cl, Br, I — all have 7 outer electrons
Reactivity decreases down group: F > Cl > Br > I
More reactive halogen displaces less reactive from its salt
Cl₂ + 2KBr → 2KCl + Br₂ (chlorine displaces bromine)
Halogens form diatomic molecules: Cl₂, Br₂, I₂

questions.question: Across a period, atomic radius...
questions.explanation: Across a period, proton number increases while electrons stay in same shell — stronger nuclear attraction pulls electrons closer.

questions.question: Moving down Group I, reactivity of alkali metals...
questions.explanation: Down Group I: outer electron is further from nucleus, less tightly held → lost more easily → more reactive.

questions.question: Sodium reacts with water producing...
questions.explanation: 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂ The product NaOH is a strong alkali — hence 'alkali metals'.

questions.question: Moving down Group VII (halogens), reactivity...
questions.explanation: Down the halogens: outer shell is further from nucleus → less easily gained → less reactive. F > Cl > Br > I

questions.question: Chlorine can displace bromide from solution because...
options: Cl is more reactive than Br and can take its electrons
questions.answer: Cl is more reactive than Br and can take its electrons
questions.explanation: Cl₂ + 2KBr → 2KCl + Br₂. More reactive halogen (Cl) displaces less reactive (Br) from its salt solution.

questions.question: Why are Group I metals stored in oil?
options: To prevent reaction with air and water
questions.answer: To prevent reaction with air and water
questions.explanation: Alkali metals are so reactive they react rapidly with water vapour and oxygen in air. Oil provides an inert protective layer.

questions.question: First ionisation energy generally increases across a period because...
options: Increasing nuclear charge with same shell → electrons held more tightly
questions.answer: Increasing nuclear charge with same shell → electrons held more tightly
questions.explanation: Across a period: same electron shell but more protons → stronger nuclear attraction → more energy needed to remove outer electron.

questions.question: Which halogen is a liquid at room temperature?
questions.explanation: Bromine (Br₂) is a dark red-brown liquid at room temperature. F₂ and Cl₂ are gases; I₂ is a solid.

questions.question: Electronegativity across a period...
questions.explanation: Electronegativity (attraction for electrons in a bond) increases across a period with increasing nuclear charge.

questions.question: Noble gases (Group 0) are unreactive because...
options: They have full outer electron shells (stable configuration)
questions.answer: They have full outer electron shells (stable configuration)
questions.explanation: Group 0 elements have 8 outer electrons (except He: 2) — completely full and stable. No tendency to gain or lose electrons.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Acids, Bases & Salts. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Acids, Bases & Salts concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Acids, Bases & Salts builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Acids, Bases & Salts. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Acids, Bases & Salts. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Acids, Bases & Salts include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Acids, Bases & Salts. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

This section of Acids, Bases & Salts builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Acids, Bases & Salts. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Acids, Bases & Salts. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Acids, Bases & Salts include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Acids, Bases & Salts. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
